blocks.html: Time to combine everything you've learned — variables, if/else, loops, lists, and functions — into a real program: a quiz game!
Since this runs without keyboard input, we'll store the "player's answers" in a list ahead of time. Try running it, then change the answers to see your score change.

blocks.starter:
questions = [
    "What color do you get by mixing red and yellow?",
    "How many legs does a spider have?",
    "What planet do we live on?"
]
correct_answers = ["orange", "8", "earth"]
player_answers  = ["orange", "8", "mars"]   # try changing these!

def check_answers(questions, correct, given):
    score = 0
    for i in range(len(questions)):
        print(questions[i])
        if given[i].lower() == correct[i].lower():
            print("✅ Correct!")
            score = score + 1
        else:
            print("❌ Not quite — the answer was", correct[i])
        print("")
    return score

final_score = check_answers(questions, correct_answers, player_answers)
print("You scored", final_score, "out of", len(questions))
blocks.label: Run Code

blocks.html: Make it your own! Change the questions and correct_answers to write your own 3-question quiz about your favorite topic (animals, space, games — anything). Then set player_answers and run it.

blocks.starter: # write your own quiz here — copy the pattern above
blocks.label: Run Code

blocks.html: 🎉 Congratulations! You've completed the PyKids foundations course. You now know enough Python to build small games, tools, and scripts. Advanced topics (files, classes, error handling) are coming soon!
